course.title: Executive Operating System™
course.subtitle: Leading, Deciding & Executing in the AI Era

slides.title: Executive Operating System™
slides.subtitle: Leading, Deciding & Executing in the AI Era
slides.body: Welcome to the Executive Operating System™. Throughout this programme you will step into the role of CEO where you will make strategic, financial, operational and transformation decisions. You will strengthen your business foundations and learn how organisations must evolve to lead, decide and execute successfully in the AI era.
slides.facilitator_notes: Welcome everyone. This is not an AI course. This is not a technology course. This is a modern business leadership programme. The objective is to help leaders understand how businesses work and how enterprises must evolve in the AI era. Throughout the programme participants will act as CEO of JourneyX and make decisions that impact Financial Health, Customer Health, People Health, Operational Health, Innovation Health and Risk & Governance Health.

slides.title: Why This Programme Exists
slides.body: Throughout my career, I have worked across strategy, sales, operations, project management, capital investment, governance and enterprise transformation. I repeatedly observed the same challenge: talented leaders often become experts in their own discipline but have limited understanding of how the rest of the business creates value. Finance leaders may not understand technology. Technology leaders may not understand finance. Functional leaders often optimise their own departments without understanding the broader enterprise. The result is slower decisions, poor alignment and missed opportunities. This programme was created to help leaders understand how businesses actually work and how they must evolve to lead, decide and execute successfully in the AI era.
slides.facilitator_notes: Tell your story rather than reading the slide. Give 2-3 examples from your own experience where teams made poor decisions because they lacked understanding of another business function. Reinforce that this course is designed to create well-rounded leaders, not specialists. Conclude with: 'The best leaders understand how the whole business works, not just their own department.' How many of you have worked with another department and realised they viewed the same problem completely differently?

slides.title: Selected Experiences
slides.body: Throughout my career, I have been fortunate to learn from industry leaders, contribute to major transformation programmes and engage with people from diverse industries and backgrounds. Every experience has reinforced one lesson: continuous learning and curiosity are essential for effective leadership.
slides.facilitator_notes: Spend 5-7 minutes introducing the photos and the stories behind them. One of the photos includes Lewis Pugh, whose story of adaptation and resilience will reappear later in the course. Do not reveal which photos are real and which are AI-generated. Build curiosity and confidence. Before moving to the next slide ask participants: 'How many of you believe you could confidently identify which of these photos are real and which are AI-generated?'

slides.title: AI-Era Leadership Lesson
slides.quote: Trust, but Verify. Seeing Is No Longer Believing. Critical thinking is a leadership capability.
slides.facilitator_notes: Reveal that Photo 4 was AI-generated. Ask participants how many selected Photo 4 correctly. Then ask: What clues did you use? What made some photos appear more authentic than others? Explain that AI can now generate highly convincing images, reports, presentations, analyses and recommendations. The lesson is not to distrust everything. The lesson is to develop critical thinking and verification habits. Leaders must increasingly make decisions in environments where content can be generated instantly and convincingly. In the AI era, trust remains important, but verification becomes essential.

slides.title: Critical Thinking in the AI Era
points: Question information before accepting it as fact.
Verify evidence and data sources before making decisions.
Challenge assumptions — including your own.
Consider alternative explanations and viewpoints.
Use AI as an advisor, not as an unquestioned authority.
Focus on judgment, context and decision quality, not just information.

Example: If AI says customer churn is increasing because of pricing, don't immediately reduce prices. Ask: What data supports this? What assumptions were made? Could there be other causes? Verify before you decide. Better information does not guarantee better decisions. Better judgment does. Related concepts: Critical Thinking • Evidence-Based Management • Decision Quality
slides.facilitator_notes: Spend 5-7 minutes on this slide. Explain that critical thinking does not mean being sceptical of everything. It means evaluating information, verifying evidence, challenging assumptions and making sound judgments before taking action. In the past, creating reports, presentations and analyses required significant effort. Today AI can generate them in seconds. The leadership challenge is no longer obtaining information; it is determining whether the information is accurate, complete, unbiased and relevant. Use the photo activity as the example. Participants saw convincing content and many were confident in their answers. Then use the customer churn example: imagine an AI assistant tells a CEO that customer churn is increasing because prices are too high. A leader with poor critical thinking immediately reduces prices. A leader with strong critical thinking asks: What evidence supports this conclusion? How reliable is the data? What other explanations exist? Is the issue pricing, service quality, competitors, product features or customer experience? Reinforce the principle: Trust, but Verify. Seeing Is No Longer Believing. Can you think of a time when a decision in your organisation was made too quickly because people trusted information without challenging it?

slides.title: Adapting to a Changing World
slides.description: As you watch this video, don't think about swimming.Think about leadership. Think about organisations. Think about change. How do leaders respond when the environment fundamentally changes?
slides.facilitator_notes: Before playing the video, tell participants that one of the photos they saw earlier was a real photo with Lewis Pugh. Briefly explain that Lewis became the first person to complete a long-distance swim at the North Pole. What was Lewis adapting to? Environment, Conditions, Risk, Uncertainty, etc. What is the equivalent for businesses today? AI & Technology, Data, Customer expectations, Competition, Speed , etc. The greatest threat to organisations isn't change. It's continuing to operate as if nothing has changed. Foundations Remain. Execution Evolves. If your organisation had to be redesigned today from scratch for the AI era, what is the first thing you would change?

slides.title: Module 1
slides.subtitle: How Businesses Must Evolve in the AI Era
slides.body: Understand why businesses must evolve, what has fundamentally changed, and the principles that should guide transformation in the AI era.

slides.title: You Are the CEO of JourneyX
slides.body:
JourneyX is one of the fastest-growing digital travel companies, offering flights, hotels, car rentals, travel insurance and loyalty rewards through a single digital platform.

Over the past three years, the company has invested more than $250 million in cloud platforms, AI, automation and data.

Yet the Board is disappointed.

• Customers still experience fragmented journeys.
• Employees wait days for reports and business insights.
• Leaders make decisions using disconnected spreadsheets.
• Teams optimise their own functions rather than the enterprise.
• Competitors are moving faster.

At the next Board meeting, the Chair asks one simple question:

'We've invested heavily in technology. Why aren't we creating more business value?'

Throughout this programme, you are the CEO.

Your job is not to buy more technology.
Your job is to build a better business.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 8-10 minutes. Ask participants: What concerns you most? What additional information would you request? Is this a technology problem, a leadership problem, a strategy problem or an operating model problem? What would you investigate first? Capture responses without providing answers. Explain that every module will provide another piece of the solution and participants will revisit JourneyX throughout the programme.

slides.title: The World Has Changed. Businesses Must Evolve.
points: Customer expectations continue to rise, demanding personalised, seamless and instant experiences.
Innovation cycles are becoming shorter while competition is increasing.
Data is growing exponentially, creating both opportunities and complexity.
AI is transforming how work is performed, decisions are made and value is created.
Regulatory, cyber security and governance requirements continue to increase.
Continuous disruption requires organisations to adapt faster than ever before.
The challenge is no longer whether change will happen. The challenge is whether organisations can evolve fast enough to respond.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 8 minutes. Ask participants which of these changes has had the greatest impact on their organisation and whether these are technology problems or business problems. Introduce Peter Drucker's quote: 'The greatest danger in times of turbulence is not the turbulence; it is to act with yesterday's logic.' Conclude that technology is not the reason organisations change—the business environment is. Technology accelerates the pace of change.

slides.title: Foundations Remain. Execution Evolves.
points: Business fundamentals such as strategy, leadership, finance, customer value and governance remain essential.
Execution evolves through trusted intelligence, AI-enabled capabilities and better decision-making.
AI augments people rather than simply automating tasks.
Workflows become more intelligent, adaptive and outcome-focused.
Successful organisations evolve how they execute, not why they exist.
Businesses don't succeed because they use AI. They succeed because they execute timeless business principles in better ways.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 10 minutes. Introduce this as the core philosophy of the Executive Operating System™. Ask participants which business foundations should never change and where their organisation is still executing with yesterday's methods. Reinforce that every remaining module will revisit this principle by showing how timeless business disciplines evolve in the AI era.

slides.title: The Executive Operating System™
points: Great organisations don't optimise for technology—they optimise for business outcomes.
Enterprise enablers work together to improve intelligence.
Better intelligence enables faster, higher-quality decisions.
Better decisions create sustainable value for customers, employees, shareholders and society.
Every investment, capability and transformation initiative should ultimately contribute to better intelligence, faster decisions and greater value creation.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 8 minutes. Introduce this as the Executive Operating System™. Explain that leadership, people, operating models, governance, data and technology are enterprise enablers. Their purpose is to improve intelligence, accelerate decision-making and create greater value. Ask participants whether their current investments can be linked to these three outcomes.

slides.title: The Executive Operating System™
slides.caption: Enterprise enablers work together to create better intelligence, enabling faster decisions and ultimately greater value creation.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 8–10 minutes. This is the signature framework of the Executive Operating System™ and will be referenced throughout the programme. Walk through the diagram from the bottom up. Explain that Leadership, People, Operating Model, Trusted Data, AI, Technology and Governance are enterprise enablers. Together they enable Better Intelligence. Better Intelligence leads to Faster Decisions. Faster Decisions create Greater Value Creation. Reinforce that technology is an enabler—not the objective. Every investment, capability and transformation initiative should ultimately improve intelligence, accelerate decision-making and create sustainable business value. Tell participants that every remaining module will strengthen one or more of these enterprise enablers.

slides.title: Three Foundations for Success in the AI Era
points: The Right Mindset — Leaders think differently before they act differently. They focus on outcomes, embrace continuous learning, apply critical thinking and lead change with confidence.
The Right Dataset — Trusted, governed and accessible data enables trusted intelligence and better decision-making across the enterprise.
The Right Toolset — AI, analytics, automation and modern collaboration tools amplify human capability and improve execution.
Mindset determines how we think. Dataset determines what we know. Toolset determines how effectively we execute.
The combination of the right mindset, the right dataset and the right toolset enables better intelligence, faster decisions and greater value creation.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 8 minutes. Reveal the three foundations after the activity. Explain that organisations often invest first in tools, but sustainable transformation starts with mindset, is enabled by trusted data, and is accelerated by the right toolset. Ask participants which of the three foundations is strongest in their organisation and which requires the greatest attention. Conclude that every remaining module in the programme strengthens one or more of these three foundations. The right mindset guides decisions. The right dataset provides trusted intelligence. The right toolset enables execution

slides.title: The Executive Operating System™
slides.caption: The right mindset, the right dataset and the right toolset enable better intelligence, faster decisions and greater value creation.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 10 minutes. This framework summarises Module 1 and introduces the Executive Operating System™ that will underpin the entire programme. Start at the bottom of the diagram by explaining the three foundations: the Right Mindset, the Right Dataset and the Right Toolset. Explain that these foundations strengthen the enterprise enablers including leadership, people, operating model, AI, technology and governance. These enablers work together to create Better Intelligence. Better Intelligence enables Faster Decisions. Faster Decisions lead to Greater Value Creation for customers, employees, shareholders and society. Reinforce that organisations should not optimise for technology alone; they should optimise for business outcomes. Conclude by explaining that every remaining module in the programme will strengthen one or more of these foundations and enterprise enablers.

slides.title: Module 2
slides.subtitle: Transforming Your Business for the AI Era
slides.facilitator_notes: Before introducing this module, return to Slide 20 (The Executive Operating System™) and spend 2–3 minutes revisiting the framework. Remind participants that the Executive Operating System™ is the foundation of the entire programme. Explain that organisations do not transform by implementing AI alone—they transform by building the right mindset, the right dataset and the right toolset, strengthening enterprise enablers, and ultimately creating better intelligence, faster decisions and greater value creation. Then introduce Module 2 by explaining that the focus now shifts from understanding why businesses must evolve to learning how to design and execute a successful transformation.

slides.title: JourneyX Is Ready to Transform. Where Should You Start?
slides.body:
Following the Board meeting, everyone agrees on one thing:

JourneyX must transform.

The executive team is eager to act, but each leader has a different opinion about where to begin.

Chief Information Officer (CIO):
'We need a modern AI platform.'

Chief Financial Officer (CFO):
'We should reduce costs before making new investments.'

Chief Marketing Officer (CMO):
'We need to improve the customer experience.'

Chief Operating Officer (COO):
'Our business processes are too slow.'

Chief Data Officer (CDO):
'Our data isn't ready.'

The CEO turns to you and asks:

'If we could only do one thing first, where should we start?'

Discussion:
What would your recommendation be—and why?
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 8–10 minutes. Encourage participants to debate the different starting points and explain their reasoning. Capture the responses without identifying a correct answer. Reinforce that successful transformation starts with executive alignment rather than immediate action. Conclude by explaining that many transformation programmes fail not because organisations lack ideas, but because leaders are not aligned on where to start. Transition to the next slide by asking: 'Why do so many transformation programmes struggle to deliver the outcomes they promise?'

Allow participants to vote before opening the discussion. Ask several participants to explain why they chose their answer. Encourage them to distinguish between root causes and symptoms. Do not reveal the answer immediately. Conclude that successful transformation begins with executive alignment around a shared vision, common principles and clear business outcomes.

slides.title: The First Step Is Not Technology
points: Research on successful transformation consistently shows that change begins with leadership alignment around a shared purpose—not technology.
John Kotter's widely adopted change leadership framework starts by creating urgency, building a guiding coalition and forming a strategic vision before execution begins.
Many organisations start with AI, new systems, automation or delivery programmes before agreeing on the business problem they are trying to solve.
Successful transformation starts by answering three executive questions:
1. What problem are we trying to solve?
2. Why does it matter now?
3. Can the executive team align on that problem before discussing solutions?
Only after leaders align on the problem should they decide how to solve it.

quote.text: Leading change is not about technology. It begins with creating urgency, building a guiding coalition and developing a vision.
quote.author: Based on John Kotter's Leading Change

slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 8–10 minutes. Refer back to the previous discussion where different executives identified different priorities—cost, AI, customer experience, delivery, processes and data. Explain that none of these perspectives is necessarily wrong; each reflects a different part of the business. The challenge is that organisations often jump straight to solutions before agreeing on the problem they are trying to solve. Introduce Kotter's work as evidence that successful transformation begins with leadership alignment around urgency and a shared vision, rather than technology. Emphasise that our methodology extends this principle by first aligning executives on the business problem before designing the future business and selecting the right solutions. Conclude with the key message: 'Don't align on the solution. Align on the problem first.' Then transition to the next slide: 'If technology isn't the starting point, what should transformation start with?'

slides.title: Technology-First vs Business-First Transformation
slides.caption: Technology enables transformation. Business design creates transformation.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 10 minutes. Explain that this comparison is not about rejecting technology—it is about choosing the right starting point. Walk through the left-hand side first, showing how many organisations begin with AI, models, chatbots and projects, hoping business value will emerge. Then explain the Business-First approach, where transformation begins with business strategy, desired outcomes and the business problems that need to be solved. Only after the operating model, enterprise capabilities and intelligence requirements are understood should AI and technology be selected. Reinforce that AI is an enabler, not the starting point. Conclude with the key message: 'Technology enables transformation. Business design creates transformation.'

slides.title: Three Questions Every Executive Team Must Answer
points: 1. What problem are we trying to solve? Identify the business challenge or opportunity before discussing solutions.
2. What outcomes do we want to achieve? Define the business outcomes that will measure success, such as better customer outcomes, faster decisions, higher productivity and greater value creation.

3. What capabilities must we build? Determine the organisational capabilities required to achieve those outcomes, including leadership, people, operating model, trusted data, AI and decision intelligence.
Technology is a means to an end—not the end itself.
Successful transformation builds the capabilities needed to achieve better business outcomes.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 8–10 minutes. Explain that executives often jump from recognising a problem directly to selecting a solution. Successful transformation follows a different path. First, align on the business problem. Second, agree on the business outcomes that define success. Third, identify the enterprise capabilities that must be built to achieve those outcomes. Only then should technology decisions be made. Reinforce that technology is an enabler of transformation, not the starting point. This thinking prepares participants for the transformation methodology introduced later in the programme.

slides.title: Don't Follow the Technology. Follow the Constraint.
slides.caption: Technology should be selected because it removes a business constraint—not because it is the latest technology.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 8–10 minutes. This is one of the core philosophies of the Executive Operating System™ and should become a recurring principle throughout the programme. Walk through the visual from top to bottom. Explain that successful transformation begins with the desired business outcome, then identifies the business constraint preventing that outcome from being achieved. Once the constraint is understood, determine the organisational capability required to remove it. Only then should the executive team evaluate which technology—including AI—is the most appropriate enabler. Reinforce that AI is not the objective; it is one possible solution. Use the JourneyX example: the CFO sees cost as the constraint, the CIO sees legacy technology, the COO sees slow delivery, the CMO sees customer experience and the CDO sees poor data quality. None of these perspectives is necessarily wrong, but the executive team's first responsibility is to agree on the most important business constraint to solve. Explain that Module 5 introduces a structured Enterprise Constraint Discovery Framework that helps organisations identify and prioritise these constraints systematically. Finish with the executive reflection: 'Before approving any AI initiative, ask: Which business constraint does this solve?'

Ask participants to select the characteristics they believe define a successful business in the AI era. Encourage discussion before revealing the answers. Ask why they selected particular characteristics and challenge assumptions around technology-focused options. Explain that the objective is not to identify organisations with the most AI, but organisations that consistently create better intelligence, faster decisions and greater value.

slides.title: Characteristics of a Successful Business in the AI Era
points: Intelligence Embedded Everywhere: Intelligence is embedded into business processes, products and decision-making rather than existing as isolated reports or specialist functions.
Decision-Making Pushed to the Edge: Decisions are made closer to customers and operations, supported by trusted intelligence and appropriate governance.
Intelligence-Empowered Teams: Teams are equipped with AI and trusted intelligence to improve judgement, collaboration and execution.
Strong Foundations: Leadership, governance, data, technology and operating models provide the foundation for sustainable transformation.
Governed Autonomy: Teams are empowered to act independently within clearly defined governance, principles and risk boundaries.
Outcome Ownership: Teams are accountable for business outcomes and value creation, rather than simply delivering activities or projects.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 10 minutes. Reveal the six characteristics and explain that these describe the organisational attributes of successful businesses in the AI era. They are not implementation steps or a maturity model; they are design principles that guide how an organisation should operate. Walk through each characteristic and relate it back to JourneyX. Explain that every remaining module in the programme will strengthen one or more of these characteristics. Strategy defines the outcomes. Leadership develops empowered teams. Technology and Data embed intelligence. Operating Models enable governed autonomy. Financial Intelligence ensures investments create value. Conclude by reinforcing that organisations should not aspire to become 'AI companies'; they should aspire to become better businesses for the AI era.

Ask participants to vote individually before opening the discussion. There is no correct answer. Encourage participants to explain why they selected their characteristic and what impact it would have on their organisation. Reinforce that every organisation has different priorities depending on its strategy, maturity and constraints. Conclude that transformation is about prioritising the capabilities that will create the greatest business value, rather than trying to improve everything simultaneously.

slides.title: Why Is It Important to Understand How Businesses Work?
points: Without understanding how businesses work as an integrated system:
Strategy becomes disconnected from execution and day-to-day decision-making.
AI initiatives become local optimisations that improve individual functions but fail to create enterprise value.
Operating models become organisational charts instead of systems that enable strategy and value creation.
Transformation becomes a collection of projects rather than a coordinated business capability.
Before transforming a business, you must first understand how a business creates value.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 8–10 minutes. Ask participants how many people in their organisation understand the whole business rather than just their own function. Explain that organisations often optimise individual departments, while customers experience one integrated business. Reinforce that successful executives think in systems rather than functions. Explain that the purpose of this module is to develop enterprise thinking by understanding how strategy, customers, operations, finance, technology, people and governance work together to create value. Conclude with the key message: 'Before transforming a business, you must first understand how a business creates value.' Transition by asking: 'What does every business actually exist to do?'

Timing: 5–7 minutes. Ask participants to vote individually before opening the discussion. Do not reveal the answer immediately. Ask several participants to explain why they selected their option. You will likely hear different perspectives such as profit, growth, shareholder value and customer focus. Explain that there have been different schools of thought throughout the history of management. Transition by saying: 'Let's see how one of the most influential management thinkers answered this question.' This leads naturally into Peter Drucker's perspective on the purpose of business.

slides.title: The Purpose of Business Hasn't Changed
points: "The purpose of business is to create and keep a customer."
— Peter Drucker
This principle has guided management thinking for decades and remains as relevant today as when it was first introduced.
In the AI era, the purpose of business remains the same.
What is changing is how businesses create and keep customers.
AI enables organisations to deliver more personalised experiences, better intelligence, faster decisions and greater customer value.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 8–10 minutes. Reveal the result of the previous poll and introduce Peter Drucker's definition of the purpose of business. Explain that many people associate business with profit, shareholder value or growth, but Drucker argued that these are outcomes of successfully creating and keeping customers rather than the purpose itself. Reinforce that this principle remains valid today. The AI era does not change why businesses exist—it changes how they fulfil that purpose. AI enables organisations to better understand customers, personalise experiences, improve decisions, anticipate needs and continuously create greater value. Conclude by reinforcing one of the core philosophies of this programme: 'Foundations Remain. Execution Evolves.' Transition to the next question: 'If the purpose of business is to create and keep customers, how do businesses actually create value?'

slides.title: How Do Businesses Create Value?
slides.caption: Michael Porter demonstrated that businesses create value through a system of interconnected activities. Competitive advantage comes from how well these activities work together.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 10–12 minutes. Explain that Michael Porter's Value Chain remains one of the most influential frameworks for understanding how businesses create value. Emphasise that businesses do not create value through a single activity; value is created through a chain of interconnected primary and supporting activities working together. Walk participants through the primary activities from inbound logistics to after-sales service, then explain how the supporting activities—firm infrastructure, human resource management, technology development and procurement—enable the entire system. Reinforce that every function contributes to customer value and competitive advantage. Do not spend time explaining every box in detail; focus on the overall concept of the business as an integrated value-creation system. Conclude by saying that while Porter's framework remains fundamental, the AI era is changing how these activities are executed. This naturally leads to the next discussion: 'If businesses create value through interconnected activities, what role does AI play in optimising those activities and creating competitive advantage?'

slides.title: How Can AI Create Greater Business Value?
slides.caption: AI creates greater business value by improving intelligence, which enables better customer experiences, smarter decisions, more capable teams and more effective operations.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 10–12 minutes. Explain that AI is not the objective—it is an enterprise capability that creates value by improving intelligence across the business. Walk participants through the framework from top to bottom. AI first improves intelligence by transforming data into insights, predictions and recommendations. Better intelligence then enables four key business outcomes: better customer experiences, smarter decisions, more capable teams and more effective operations. Together, these outcomes create greater business value through improved growth, performance, resilience and competitive advantage. Reinforce that AI should never be viewed as a standalone technology initiative. Its value is realised only when it strengthens business capabilities and contributes to measurable business outcomes. Connect this back to the Executive Operating System™ by reinforcing the relationship: Better Intelligence → Better Decisions → Greater Business Value. Transition by asking: 'If AI can improve every activity across the value chain, how should organisations redesign the way they operate to fully realise that value?'

slides.title: Why Local Optimisation Isn't Enough
points: AI can create significant value within individual business functions.
However, optimising one function rarely optimises the entire business.
Competitive advantage comes from improving how business capabilities work together—not independently.
The greatest value is created when intelligence flows seamlessly across the enterprise.
Enterprise transformation requires optimising the whole system, not just individual departments.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 8–10 minutes. Begin with a simple example using JourneyX. Imagine JourneyX invests heavily in AI for Customer Service. Customers receive faster responses and lower waiting times, but Marketing cannot access customer insights, Product teams do not learn from customer feedback, Operations cannot anticipate demand, Finance cannot improve forecasting, and Leadership still makes slow decisions. Ask participants: 'Has JourneyX transformed its business?' Most will conclude that only one capability has improved. Explain that this is local optimisation. While valuable, it does not create enterprise transformation or sustainable competitive advantage. Reinforce that AI delivers its greatest value when intelligence is shared across business capabilities and supports end-to-end value creation. Conclude with the key message: 'Optimising one function improves a department. Optimising the whole system transforms the business.' Transition by saying: 'This idea is not new. Long before AI, one management thinker argued that organisations should be understood as systems rather than collections of individual departments.'

slides.title: Systems Thinking: The Business as an Integrated System
points: "The performance of a system depends on how well its parts work together—not how well each part performs individually."
— Inspired by Peter Senge's Systems Thinking
Businesses are complex systems where customers, people, processes, technology, data and finance continuously influence one another.
Improving individual functions creates local optimisation. Improving the relationships between functions creates enterprise value.
In the AI era, intelligence must flow across the entire business—not remain isolated within departments.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 10–12 minutes. Introduce Peter Senge and explain that Systems Thinking transformed the way leaders understand organisations. Rather than viewing a business as a collection of departments, Systems Thinking encourages leaders to see it as an interconnected system where every capability influences the others. Refer back to the JourneyX example from the previous slide and explain that optimising Customer Service alone did not transform the business because the other capabilities remained disconnected. Reinforce that AI increases the importance of Systems Thinking because intelligence becomes significantly more valuable when it is shared across the enterprise. Explain that isolated AI solutions create isolated improvements, whereas connected intelligence enables coordinated decisions, better collaboration and greater business value. Conclude with the key message: 'Businesses compete as integrated systems, not as collections of independent functions.' Transition by saying: 'This brings us to the evolution of management thinking and how these foundational ideas come together in the Executive Operating System™.' Introduce the book: the fifth discipline by Peter Senge as a recommended resource for participants interested in learning more about Systems Thinking.

slides.title: From Business Foundations to the Executive Operating System™
slides.caption: The Executive Operating System™ builds on proven management thinking, evolving it for the AI era.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 10–12 minutes. Use this slide to connect everything participants have learned throughout the module. Explain that the Executive Operating System™ does not replace established management thinking—it builds upon it. Peter Drucker taught us why businesses exist: to create and keep customers. Michael Porter explained how businesses create value through interconnected activities. The AI era introduces a new enterprise capability by embedding intelligence into those activities, enabling better customer experiences, smarter decisions, more capable teams and more effective operations. Peter Senge's Systems Thinking reminds us that sustainable competitive advantage comes from optimising the whole business system rather than individual functions. Explain that the Executive Operating System™ integrates these foundations into a practical management system for leading and executing in the AI era. Reinforce the central philosophy of the programme: 'Foundations Remain. Execution Evolves.' Mention that participants interested in Systems Thinking may find Peter Senge's book 'The Fifth Discipline' valuable for further reading. Conclude by explaining that the remaining modules will progressively build each capability of the Executive Operating System™, providing practical tools, frameworks and methodologies for transforming organisations in the AI era.

slides.title: Value Depends on Perspective
slides.description: Businesses create value for multiple stakeholders, and each stakeholder defines value differently.
rows: Better outcomes, convenience, quality, trust, lower cost, great experiences
Revenue growth, profitability, brand recognition, customer loyalty, customer satisfaction, sustainable returns
Meaningful work, growth and development, recognition, wellbeing, fair compensation
Successful collaboration, reliability, shared growth, trust, mutual success
Social impact, sustainability, ethical behaviour, responsible business practices
Compliance, transparency, security, reduced risk, consumer protection
slides.footer: There is no single definition of value. Value depends on whose perspective you consider.
slides.facilitator_notes: Timing: 8–10 minutes. Introduce the idea that value is not universal; it depends on who receives it. Walk through each stakeholder and discuss what they consider valuable. Emphasise that while organisations create value for many stakeholders, different stakeholders measure value differently. Ask participants: 'Which stakeholder does your organisation prioritise most today? Has that changed over time?' Allow a short discussion before concluding that although businesses create value for many stakeholders, customer value is the foundation because customers enable value creation for everyone else. Transition to the next slide by asking: 'If value depends on perspective, why do successful businesses place such a strong emphasis on customer value?'

activities.title: Real or AI?
activities.question: Looking at the five photos in the collage, which photo do you believe is AI-generated?

activities.title: What Is Really Holding JourneyX Back?
activities.question: Based on the information you've seen so far, what do you believe is the primary reason JourneyX isn't creating the expected business value?
options: I don't have enough information yet

activities.title: What Changes... and What Doesn't?
activities.question: Drag each item into the category where you believe it belongs.

activities.title: Enterprise Outcomes or Enterprise Enablers?
activities.question: Every organisation invests in many initiatives. Some are enterprise outcomes the organisation wants to achieve, while others are enablers that help achieve those outcomes. Drag each item into the correct category.

activities.title: What Are the Three Foundations for Success in the AI Era?
activities.question: Based on everything you have learned in this module, identify the three essential foundations for success in the AI era.

activities.title: What Is the Biggest Barrier to Successful Business Transformation?
activities.question: Based on your experience, what is the biggest barrier preventing organisations from successfully transforming their business for the AI era?

activities.title: Which Characteristics Define a Successful Business in the AI Era?
activities.question: Select the SEVEN characteristics that best define a successful business in the AI era.

activities.title: Which Characteristic Should Your Organisation Prioritise?
